flags: The existing steps had the assembly order backwards: they build a rectangle out of the four 23-3/4" 'Frame ends' in 'all four corners', then add the 26-1/2" side rails afterward. Four equal-length ends cannot make a rectangle. The only reading the cut list supports is two 26-1/2" side rails running the full height with the five 23-3/4" cross members (four 1x2 ends plus the 1x3 center bar) between them.

FASTENER GAP, real: with the 1x2 rails face-forward — the only orientation a clothespin can be glued to — a brad driven straight in through a rail's outside edge has to cross the rail's 1-1/2" width before it reaches the cross member. The plan's only nail is the 1-1/4" brad, which cannot. The rewrite glues every butt joint and toenails through the rail's 3/4" back face instead; a longer fastener is not in the materials list so none is named.
Overall size is DERIVED, not authored: 23-3/4" cross member plus two 1-1/2" rail faces = 26-3/4" wide, by 26-1/2" tall (the rail length).

The clothespin layout is under-specified. The plan gives 12 clothespins and a 6-1/2"-from-center spacing but never says how many cross members carry them; 12 at three per member covers four of the five.
Lumber verified sufficient, not short: the six 1x2 parts pack into the two declared 8 ft boards with 19" of slack.

ops.title: Assemble the outer frame
ops.body: The two 26-1/2" side rails run the full height of the frame and every 23-3/4" cross member fits between them, so start by joining one frame end across the top and one across the bottom to make the outer rectangle. Lay the 1x2s face-forward — wide 1-1/2" face toward you — because that face is what the clothespins glue to later, and it is what makes the finished frame 26-3/4" wide by 26-1/2" tall. Glue every butt joint and clamp it, then pin each corner by driving 1-1/4" brads at an angle through the rail's 3/4"-thick back face into the end of the cross member; driven straight in from the outside edge a 1-1/4" brad would have to cross the rail's full 1-1/2" width and would never reach. Measure both diagonals from opposite outside corners and adjust until they match before the glue sets — on a plain open rectangle an out-of-square corner is the only thing there is to look at.

ops.title: Add the interior cross members
ops.body: Three cross members remain: the two leftover 23-3/4" 1x2 frame ends and the 23-3/4" 1x3 center bar. Space them across the 26-1/2" opening — the 1x3 center bar in the middle, where its 2-1/2" face gives the widest landing for a clothespin, and a 1x2 end between it and each of the top and bottom rails. Mark both side rails together from the same end so each cross member lands square, then glue and toenail two 1-1/4" brads per joint through the back of the rail, the same way you fastened the corners.

ops.title: Stain and add the clothespins
ops.body: Stain the whole frame and let it dry fully before any glue touches it — staining around glued-on clothespins is miserable and the glue squeeze-out will resist the stain. Glue the clothespins to the front faces of the cross members with the jaws pointing up, one on the centerline of each member and one 6-1/2" out to each side; the twelve clothespins cover four members at three apiece. Press each one down until glue squeezes out and leave the frame flat until it cures, so nothing slides while it sets.

baseStepTitles: Attach the back cleat to the wall
Attach the legs to the cleat
flags: THE BACK CLEAT IS NOT IN THE CUT LIST. The description and two steps require it, but no cutList row produces it, and all nine declared 2x4 are consumed exactly by the listed parts (packBoards: 9 boards for the 3x37-3/4", 3x10-1/2" and 7x75" pieces, zero spare). So a tenth 2x4 is needed. The rewrite says so and sizes the cleat to the 75" slat length, which is the only width the plan states.

SCREW COUNT SHORT: the only 2-1/2" wood screws are the 16 listed 'for wall attachment', but the slats alone take two per leg on seven slats across three legs = 42, before a single screw goes into the wall. Buy more.
The 2x4 count is NOT a shortfall for the listed parts — 9 needed, 9 declared. It is exactly enough, with no board to spare and none left for the cleat.
Slat spacing is DERIVED, not authored: the 37-3/4" leg back piece sets the depth, seven 3-1/2" slats consume 24-1/2", leaving 13-1/4" over six gaps, about 2-1/4" each.
The 14" leg cap equals the 10-1/2" base plus the 3-1/2" width of the back piece, which is the only placement that closes; the plan never states it.

ops.title: Build the three L-shaped legs
ops.body: Each leg is an L: a 37-3/4" 2x4 back piece with a 10-1/2" 2x4 base butted square to one end, joined with two 2-1/2" pocket hole screws driven from the base into the back piece. Note that 10-1/2" plus the 3-1/2" width of the back piece is exactly 14" — the length of the 1x2 leg cap — so the cap lies across the outside of the L covering both members and pinning the joint; glue it and add 1-1/4" brads. Build all three legs the same way and stack them together to check they match; three legs that differ leave the slats sitting on a twist.

ops.title: Attach the back cleat to the wall
ops.body: The back cleat is not in this plan's cut list and the nine declared 2x4 are fully consumed by the legs and slats, so buy one more 8 ft 2x4 and cut the cleat to 75", matching the slat length that sets the daybed's width. Find the studs, mark a level line at your chosen seat height, and screw the cleat flat to the wall with the 2-1/2" wood screws, at least two into every stud it crosses. Get this line level and straight before anything else attaches to it — every leg and every slat inherits it, and a cleat that sags in the middle cannot be corrected later.
materials: Wood screws, 2-1/2" or longer, for wall attachment

ops.title: Attach the legs to the cleat
ops.body: Set one leg at each end of the 75" cleat and one in the middle, roughly 37-1/2" on center, so no slat spans more than about three feet unsupported. Attach each leg's back piece to the cleat with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, keeping the top edges flush so the slats land on one plane. At least one leg and preferably both outer legs must land on a wall stud — that stud connection is what stops the daybed racking sideways when someone sits on the front edge, and drywall anchors alone will not hold it.

ops.title: Attach the slats
ops.body: Lay the seven 75" slats across the three legs, running the full width. Seven slats at 3-1/2" wide consume 24-1/2" of the 37-3/4" depth set by the leg back pieces, which leaves 13-1/4" to divide over six gaps — about 2-1/4" between slats. Screw each slat down with two 2-1/2" screws into every leg it crosses; that is 42 screws for the slats alone, so plan on more than the sixteen the materials list carries. If you want a more finished look than bare slat ends, cover the exposed ends with plywood or planking pinned on with 1-1/4" brads — that is cosmetic only and carries no load.
materials: Wood screws, 2-1/2" or longer, for wall attachment

baseStepTitles: Drill pocket holes and build the sides
Join the sides with the top supports
Build and install the mirrored door
flags: TWO REAL SHORTFALLS, both provable by packBoards. 1x2: the four 44" bottom drawer trims, four 23-1/4" smaller drawer trims and the 49" top trim total 318" and pack into FOUR 8 ft boards; materials declare 2. 1x3: 331-1/2" of parts pack into FOUR boards with zero slack; materials declare 3. The existing cut step already says four of each — it is the materials list that is wrong, and the shopping list will come up short.
THE 56" CENTER DIVIDER IS NEVER INSTALLED BY ANY STEP. The steps jump from trimming the bottom shelf straight to 'Add the center divider trim'. Folded into step 7 in this rewrite.
Step 2's body said to attach '1x2 side trim' to the panels, but the cut list part is 'Side trim, top and bottom' in Pine 1x3 and that step's own materials list 1x3. Corrected to 1x3.
Step 13 called for '1-1/4" finish nails'. There is no finish nail in the materials; the list carries '1-1/4" brad nails'. Routed to the brad nails.

Verified sound and worth keeping: the large drawer bay is BELOW the bottom shelf (67" side less the 56" divider = 11" of case under it, and the large face is 43-3/4" wide, which cannot live in either upper bay); the 56" divider standing on the shelf splits the 44" interior less its 1-1/2" 2x2 trim into 23-1/4" and 19-1/4", matching the 23-1/4" upper shelf and drawer trim and the 19" door; the door closes exactly — 14" rails plus two 2-1/2" stiles = 19" wide, 54-1/4" less two 2-1/2" rails = 49-1/4", which is the mirror.
Do not assert where the 69" legs sit relative to the 67" sides, or how the 49" top overhangs — the cut list does not determine either.

ops.title: Drill pocket holes and build the sides
ops.body: This armoire is built to a specific mirror and to drawer faces ripped from 1x10 at 9-1/4" wide, so read the whole plan before cutting into it. Drill every pocket hole for the entire project now, while the stock is still flat — going back to drill inside an assembled 67"-tall case is far harder than doing it on the bench. Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the side edges of both 67" side panels, then attach the four 15-3/4" 1x3 side trim pieces across the top and bottom of each panel, flush with the panel edges.

ops.title: Join the sides with the top supports
ops.body: Stand the two side assemblies parallel and join them with the two 44" 2x2 top supports, one at the front and one at the back, running between the sides at the top. That 44" span is the case's interior width and every part above it depends on it — the bottom shelf, the bottom drawer trim and the large drawer face are all cut to it. Check both diagonals across the open case and clamp it square before driving the screws.

ops.title: Add the bottom shelf
ops.body: The 44" bottom shelf spans between the two sides and is what divides the case: everything above it is the shelved and mirrored compartments, and the 11" of case left below it is the large drawer bay. Position it so the 56" center divider standing on top of it reaches the top of the 67" sides, then fasten it through pocket holes into both side panels. Before it goes in, drill 3/4" pocket holes along its front and back edges pointing outward — those are for the bottom drawer trim in the next step, and you cannot reach them once the shelf is captured.

ops.title: Trim the bottom shelf
ops.body: The four 44" 1x2 bottom drawer trims frame the large drawer bay: two on the front, above and below the opening, and two matching them on the back. Attach them through the pocket holes you drilled in the shelf edges with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, plus glue and 1-1/4" brad nails where a screw cannot reach. Keep the front pair perfectly parallel — they are what the 43-3/4" drawer face registers its 1/8" reveal against, and any taper in the opening shows up as a wedge-shaped gap.

ops.title: Install the center divider and its trim
ops.body: Stand the 56" center divider panel on the bottom shelf, running front to back, and fasten it up through pocket holes into the shelf and into the top support. It does not sit centered: the 44" interior less the 1-1/2" 2x2 divider trim leaves 23-1/4" on one side for the shelved and drawered compartment and 19-1/4" on the other for the mirrored door, and those two figures are what the 23-1/4" upper shelf and the 19"-wide door are cut to. Then add the two 54-1/2" 2x2 center divider trims to the divider's front and back edges, keeping them flush to the inside of the larger drawer compartment — that flush face is the reference the drawer trim and faces all measure from.

ops.title: Add the smaller drawer trim
ops.body: The four 23-1/4" 1x2 smaller drawer trims divide the 23-1/4"-wide compartment into the three small drawer openings, two on the front and two matching on the back. Space them so each opening suits a 9-1/4"-wide drawer face with a 1/8" gap on all sides — that is a 9-1/2" clear opening per drawer. Attach with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and check each opening's width at both ends before moving on; the boxes in a later step are built 1" narrower than the opening and there is no slack to absorb a taper.

ops.title: Add the upper shelf
ops.body: The 23-1/4" upper shelf closes the top of the drawered compartment and forms the floor of the open shelved cubby above it. Drill 3/4" pocket holes on all four edges of the shelf, then set it level between the side panel and the center divider and screw it home. Its length matches the compartment exactly, so dry-fit it before driving anything — if it fights, the divider is out of plumb rather than the shelf being long.

ops.title: Build and install the drawers
ops.body: Build four boxes: three small ones from the 16" sides and 20-3/4" front and back pieces, and one large one from the 41-1/2" sides and 16" front and back pieces. In every box the 16" pieces run the full length and the other pair fits between them, which the 1/4" bottoms confirm — a small box lands at 22-1/4" outside (20-3/4" plus two 3/4" ends) to match its 22-1/4" bottom, and the large box at 43" to match its 43" bottom. That leaves exactly 1" in the 23-1/4" small openings and in the 44" large opening, which is the clearance the Euro slides need. Keep the pocket holes on the outside faces where the drawer face will hide them, and mount the boxes inset 3/4" back from the front of the case so the faces sit proud.
materials: 16" Euro-style bottom corner mount drawer slides, pair

ops.title: Build and install the mirrored door
ops.body: The door frame closes exactly on the mirror, so build it to the numbers: two 54-1/4" 1x3 stiles with the two 14" 1x3 rails between them gives a door 19" wide by 54-1/4" tall — matching the 19" x 54-1/4" backer panel — and the field left inside is 14" wide by 49-1/4" tall, which is the mirror. Join the frame with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws, then pin the 1/4" backer to the back of the frame with 3/4" brad nails. Hang the door on the narrow profile hinges in the 19-1/4" opening and set the mirror into the field only after the door swings true.

ops.title: Attach the drawer faces
ops.body: The faces are sized so the reveal falls out on its own: each 23" small face in a 23-1/4" opening and the 43-3/4" large face in the 44" opening both leave 1/8" all round. Center each face over its closed drawer box on 1/8" spacers, then fix it with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails driven from inside the box — the plan's fastener here is a brad, not a finish nail. Finally drill for and fit the knobs or handles on the four faces and the mirrored door.

baseStepTitles: Build the main side panels and legs
Add top supports and the middle shelf
Build the corner add-on side panel
Add the corner add-on shelves and top
flags: 1x2 SHORTFALL, provable: 228-1/2" of 1x2 parts pack into THREE 8 ft boards; the materials list declares 2. The existing cut step already says three — the materials line is what is wrong.

THE TWO BACK PANELS APPEAR TO BE LABELLED BACKWARDS. 'Back panel, main' is 19" x 27" and 'Back panel, corner add-on' is 31-1/2" x 27". The main unit is 32-1/4" wide overall and the add-on bay is only 16-1/2" clear, so the 31-1/2" panel belongs to the main unit and the 19" panel to the add-on. Both are 27" tall, which suits either. Stated in the prose, not silently swapped.

The legs are flush to the INSIDE face of the side panels. That is the only reading that closes: it makes the clear span between assemblies 29-1/4", which is exactly the length of the shelves, the shelf trim and the 2x2 top and bottom trims, and gives 32-1/4" overall under the 32-3/4" top (1/4" of overhang each end). Outer-flush would push the span to 30-3/4" and the 29-1/4" shelves would not reach.

The corner add-on's own side panel stands 16-1/2" clear of the main unit's side, so it cannot simply be screwed to it as the old step 5 said — the four 16-1/2" 2x2 top and bottom trims are the actual structural connection between the two units.
1x12 and 2x2 verified sufficient: 2 and 4 boards respectively, matching what is declared.
The 29" legs are 1" longer than the 26-1/2" panel plus its two 3/4" trims (28"); the plan does not say where that inch goes, so the rewrite does not assert it.

ops.title: Build the main side panels and legs
ops.body: Each side assembly is one 26-1/2" x 11-1/4" panel with an 11-1/4" 1x2 trim across its top edge and another across its bottom, then a 29" 2x2 leg at the front edge and another at the back. Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the panel's front and back edges before the legs go on, and attach each leg so its INSIDE face is flush with the inside face of the panel — the leg then stands proud on the outside. That flush inside face is the reference for the whole unit: it puts the two assemblies 29-1/4" apart, which is exactly the length every shelf and trim piece is cut to. Build both side assemblies identically and stack them to check before going on.

ops.title: Add top and bottom trim and the middle shelf
ops.body: Stand the two side assemblies parallel, 29-1/4" apart inside face to inside face, and join them with the four 29-1/4" 2x2 top and bottom trims — two across the top, front and back, and two across the bottom. Then glue and pin a 29-1/4" 1x2 middle shelf trim to the front edge of each 29-1/4" shelf and fasten the trimmed shelves between the panels with pocket hole screws driven from underneath so nothing shows. Check the diagonals as you go: with a 29-1/4" span every part is cut to one number, so a rack of even 1/8" makes the last shelf fight.

ops.title: Attach the top
ops.body: Glue and pin the two 32-3/4" 1x2 top trims to the front and back edges of the 32-3/4" top, then set the top on the case. The unit measures 32-1/4" across the outside faces of the legs, so a 32-3/4" top centered on it leaves a 1/4" reveal at each end — take that off both ends rather than letting it all land on one side. Fasten down through pocket holes in the top supports so the top face stays clean. The standalone main bookshelf is complete at this point; stop here if you do not want the corner section.

ops.title: Build and set the corner add-on side panel
ops.body: Build the add-on's third side panel exactly like the main ones: a 26-1/2" panel with two 11-1/4" 1x2 trims and a 29" 2x2 leg flush to its inside face at each edge. Stand it 16-1/2" clear of the main unit's side panel, at right angles to the main unit's run so the two sections wrap the corner. It does not attach to the main unit directly — 16-1/2" of air separates them — so brace it plumb for now and let the trims in the next step make the connection.

ops.title: Tie the add-on together with trims, shelves and top
ops.body: The four 16-1/2" 2x2 top and bottom trims are what actually joins the add-on to the main unit: two across the top and two across the bottom, running from the new side panel to the main unit's side, fastened with pocket hole screws at both ends. Then add the two 16-1/2" shelves in the same order as the main unit, and finish with the 18-1/2" top carrying its two 18-1/2" 1x2 trims. Keep the add-on's shelf heights level with the main unit's — matching lines are what makes the two sections read as one continuous shelf rather than two boxes shoved into a corner.

ops.title: Attach the backs and finish
ops.body: The two optional 1/4" backs are both 27" tall but different widths, and the cut list's labels look reversed: the 31-1/2" panel matches the main unit's 32-1/4" width, and the 19" panel matches the add-on's 16-1/2" bay. Hold each panel up to its opening before nailing and use whichever actually fits. Glue and pin them on with 1-1/4" brads, checking the case for square first — a back panel locks in whatever shape the case is in when it goes on. Then sand every surface and apply primer and paint, or stain and poly.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

baseStepTitles: Build and bolt on the guardrail
Add the batten trim and finish
flags: The cut step said 'five 8 ft 2x2' against four declared. FOUR IS ENOUGH and this is not a shortfall: two boards each take a 39" panel top plus two 24" legs (87-1/4" with kerfs) and the other two take one 75" cleat each. FFD's fifth board is a binning artifact. Corrected to four.

Step 3 offered 'a 2" nail works' as an alternative. THERE IS NO 2" FASTENER IN THE MATERIALS LIST — only 1-1/4" and 2-1/2" pocket screws, 1-1/4" wood screws and 1-1/4" finish nails. Removed rather than substituted.

REAL AND IMPORTANT: the 1x3 material note promises 'mattress slats' from the same boards, but no two of the four guardrail pieces (75", 72", 63-1/2", 60-1/2") share a 96" board, so all four 1x3 are consumed and the longest offcut is 96 - 60-1/2 - 1/8 = 35-3/8". A slat must cross the 39" interior width. The 1x3 cannot supply the slats; buy the bunkie board or separate slat lumber.

Verified sound and now stated in the prose: guardrail 72" bottom plus two 1-1/2" ends = 75", matching the side rail; headboard 39" plus two 1-1/2" legs = 42" overall; baluster gaps (72 - 14 x 1-1/2)/15 = about 3-3/8" and (60-1/2 - 21)/15 = about 2-5/8"; batten gaps (39 - 5 x 1-1/2)/4 = 7-7/8"; each 10 ft 1x6 yields one 75" rail plus one 39" trim with 5-7/8" left.

ops.title: Cut all the parts
ops.body: Cut two panels at 39" from the 3/4 in plywood. Cut two panel tops at 39", four legs at 24" and two cleats at 75" from the 2x2. Cut two panel bottom trim at 39" and two side rails at 75" from the 1x6. Cut 28 guardrail rails at 8", four guardrail ends at 16" and ten headboard trim battens at 16-1/4" from the 1x2. Cut one guardrail top (ladder side) at 63-1/2", one guardrail top (back side) at 75", one guardrail bottom (ladder side) at 60-1/2" and one guardrail bottom (back side) at 72" from the 1x3. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to four 8 ft 2x2 (two boards each take a 39" panel top and two 24" legs, the other two take one 75" cleat apiece), two 10 ft 1x6 (one 75" rail and one 39" trim per board, leaving 5-7/8"), six 8 ft 1x2 and four 8 ft 1x3. Note that no two of the four 1x3 guardrail pieces fit on one 96" board, so all four are consumed and the longest offcut is 35-3/8" — not long enough to cross the 39" bunk, so the mattress slats have to come from the bunkie board or slat lumber, not from these. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

ops.title: Add the legs
ops.body: Attach a 24" leg to each end of both panel assemblies with glue and 1-1/4" pocket screws through the holes you drilled into the 3/4" panel stock. Where the leg meets the 2x2 panel top, use a pocket hole set for 1-1/2" stock and a 2-1/2" screw — that joint carries real load and the deeper screw is what it needs. A 39" panel with a 1-1/2" leg at each end makes each headboard 42" wide overall, so check that figure across both assemblies before the glue sets.

ops.title: Build and bolt on the guardrail
ops.body: There are two guardrails, and each is a ladder frame: the top rail runs the full length and the bottom rail sits between the two 16" ends, which is why the back-side pair is 75" over 72" and the ladder-side pair 63-1/2" over 60-1/2" — the 3" difference is the two 1-1/2" ends. Fourteen 8" balusters go in each: over the 72" back-side bottom that leaves 51" across fifteen gaps, about 3-3/8" each, and over the 60-1/2" ladder-side bottom about 2-5/8" each. Drill one pocket hole on the inside of every baluster and one on the top of each end, plus two in the ends of each bottom rail, and assemble the frames flat. Then bolt the finished guardrails to the side rails with bolts, nuts and washers placed symmetrically — never screws. A guardrail is the one part on a top bunk that must not work loose, which is exactly why it gets a mechanical fastener you can retighten.

ops.title: Add the mattress support
ops.body: Screw the two 75" 2x2 cleats to the inside faces of the 1x6 side rails, level with each other and set down far enough that the mattress top ends up below the guardrail. Then lay a bunkie board across them, or slats spaced to your mattress maker's recommendation. Note that the four 1x3 boards are entirely consumed by the guardrails and their longest offcut is 35-3/8", which will not span the 39" width — so the slats come from the bunkie board or separate slat lumber. Screw slats down with 1-1/4" wood screws and NO glue so they lift out for cleaning and mattress changes.

ops.title: Add the batten trim and finish
ops.body: Five 16-1/4" battens go on each panel, and their length is not arbitrary — it is exactly the plywood left showing between the 2x2 top and the 5-1/2" 1x6 bottom trim. Across the 39" panel, five 1-1/2" battens consume 7-1/2", leaving 31-1/2" over four gaps, so space them 7-7/8" apart; run a story stick or mark both panels together so the two ends match. Glue each batten and pin it with 1-1/4" finish nails. Then fill the nail holes, sand everything smooth — especially the guardrail, which small hands grip — and paint or finish.

baseStepTitles: Attach the sides to the side trim
Add the legs and front/back trim
flags: HEADLINE DEFECT — THE FRONT IS OVER-SUBSCRIBED AND THE DRAWER DOES NOT FIT. The face between the legs is 54-1/2", less the two 1-1/2" 2x2 drawer/door trims leaves 51-1/2" of opening. The cut list's two doors at 15-3/8" plus a drawer box built from the 20-1/4" front and back pieces come to 51", leaving 1/2" total — which the doors' own 1/8" reveals consume by themselves, before a single side guide (3/4" per side off a 1x2, or 1-1/2" off a 2x2) goes in. The plan cannot be built as drawn. The rewrite says so at the point the parts get used instead of writing 'build to your real opening' over the top of it.

THE TOP DOES NOT OVERHANG AT THE ENDS. The legs sit flush to the inside of the box — that is exactly what lets the 54-1/2" front and back trims fit between them — so each leg projects 3/4" past the 56" box, making the width at the legs 57-1/2", which is the top's length exactly. Flush, not a reveal.

The 1x2 has only about 20" of usable offcut across its two declared boards after the side trim, top trim back and door battens, which is nowhere near enough for the drawer bottom guides the plan calls for.

FASTENER GAP: the only screw in the materials list is the 2-1/2" wood screw. Step 3 called for 1-1/4" pocket screws and step 6 for 2" screws; neither exists in the list, and a 2-1/2" screw driven into 3/4" stock will blow through. The rewrite names the joint and the problem rather than inventing a fastener.
Verified fine and not flagged as shortfalls: 1x12 226" into 3 boards, 1x8 127" of 144", 1x2 172" into 2, 2x2 4 boards of 5 declared, 1x3 2 of 2.

ops.title: Build the box
ops.body: Assemble the two 19-1/2" sides, the 57-1/2" top, the 54-1/2" bottom and the two 18-3/4" partitions into a box: the bottom runs between the sides, so the carcass finishes 56" wide over its outside faces. Set the two partitions to divide the interior into the two door bays and the center drawer bay. Pocket screws sized for 3/4" stock are the cleanest joint here — note the materials list only carries 2-1/2" screws, which will blow straight through 3/4" pine, so buy short pocket screws or fall back to countersunk screws and glue. Check both diagonals before the glue grabs; every door and drawer downstream is fighting for half an inch and a racked box takes that half inch away.

ops.title: Add the legs and front/back trim
ops.body: Attach the four 23-1/4" legs flush to the inside of the box and the outside of the side trim, so each leg projects 3/4" past the 56" carcass — that puts the width across the legs at 57-1/2", which is the length of the top, so the top finishes flush at the ends rather than overhanging. Then run the four 54-1/2" 2x2 front and back trims between the legs, two at the front and two at the back, and tie the legs into the trim with glue and screws or pocket holes from inside. That 54-1/2" between the legs is the whole front of the console, and everything on the face has to fit inside it.

ops.title: Finish the top trim
ops.body: Glue and pin the 57-1/2" 1x2 to the back edge of the top and the 57-1/2" 1x3 to the front edge; both run the full length of the top, matching it end to end. If you are using pocket holes, drill them in the underside of the 1x12 top and pull the trim up from below so nothing shows on the finished surface. Keep the front trim's top face dead level with the top — a proud edge on a media console catches every remote and cable that slides across it.

ops.title: Build the drawers and guides — and check the face first
ops.body: Before cutting a drawer part, measure the finished face: it is 54-1/2" between the legs, and the two 16-1/2" 2x2 drawer/door trims that split it into three bays take 3" of that, leaving 51-1/2" of actual opening. The cut list does not fit inside it — two 15-3/8" doors plus a box built from the 20-1/4" front and back pieces total 51", and the doors' own 1/8" reveals eat the remaining 1/2" before any guide is added, so the drawer as drawn cannot be installed. Divide your real 51-1/2" among the three bays first, then cut the drawer front and back to the center bay less the guides you intend to use (3/4" per side off a 1x2, 1-1/2" off a 2x2) and the 11-1/2" sides to your case depth. Set the bottom guides first, shimming up about 1/8" until the drawer runs level, then add the side guides; also note the 1x2 has only about 20" of offcut left across its two boards, so plan the guide stock before you cut.

ops.title: Build the doors, then finish
ops.body: Each door is two 1x3 rails top and bottom, three 1x2 battens between them, and a 1/4" plywood back — the cut list draws them at 15-3/8" wide by 16-1/4" tall, but cut them to whatever the two outer bays actually measured in the last step, less 1/4" in each direction for an even 1/8" reveal all round. Glue and brad the battens to the plywood back, then pin the rails on, keeping the frame square so the door does not sit twisted in its opening. Hang the doors, fit the four knobs or handles across the doors and drawers, then fill, sand and finish — the board-and-batten face takes paint and primer beautifully, and paint also hides the plywood door backs' edge grain.

baseStepTitles: Build and attach the face frame
flags: 1x12 SHORTFALL, hand-proved: the 72", 69-1/2", two 35-1/4", two 34-3/8" and 31-1/2" pieces cannot be paired any better than one long piece per board (72 leaves 24, 69-1/2 leaves 26-1/2, and the smallest remaining part is 31-1/2"), so FIVE 8 ft boards are required against FOUR declared. The existing cut step already says five; the materials line is what is wrong.

FOUR DOORS, TWO OPENINGS — genuinely unbuildable as written, and NOT resolved here. The face frame gives exactly two openings: the 66" footer sits between the two 2-1/2" sides, less the 2-1/2" center divider trim leaves 63-1/2" over two bays, so each opening is 31-3/4" wide by 30-1/4" tall (32-3/4" side less the 2-1/2" footer). The cut list nonetheless lists 4 doors, 4 pairs of hinges and 4 knobs, at 15-1/2" x 15" — which is neither half nor a quarter of an opening with any consistent reveal. The plan does not say whether the four doors are two pairs sharing the two openings or something else. Flagged, not guessed.
CENTER DIVIDER TRIM LENGTH IS WRONG: it is listed at 27-1/4" but must span the same 30-1/4" as the openings it separates (32-3/4" face frame side less the 2-1/2" footer). Corrected in the prose.
THE TOP OVERHANGS 1/2" PER END, NOT 3/4": the 69-1/2" bottom shelf between two 3/4" sides makes the carcass 71" wide, matching the 71" face frame header and the 71" back panel, and the top is 72".
BASE MOULDING IS SHORT: four 20" pieces (80" total) cannot wrap a 72"-wide by 11-1/4"-deep base, which needs about 94-1/2" plus miter waste — more than the single 8 ft stick declared.

Verified sound: 1x3 needs 3 of the 6 declared boards for the face frame, leaving 3 for doors; the 34-3/8" interior shelves match (69-1/2 less the 3/4" divider, halved); the 36" x 71" back matches the 35-1/4" sides plus the 3/4" top.

ops.title: Cut all the parts
ops.body: Cut one top at 72", two sides at 35-1/4", one center divider at 31-1/2", one bottom shelf at 69-1/2" and two interior shelves at 34-3/8" from the Pine 1x12. Cut one back panel at 71" x 36" from the 1/4" plywood. Cut one top front trim at 72" from the Pine 1x2. From the Pine 1x3, cut two face frame sides at 32-3/4", one face frame header at 71", one face frame footer at 66" — and cut the center divider trim at 30-1/4", NOT the 27-1/4" the cut list shows, because it has to span the same height as the openings it divides (32-3/4" less the 2-1/2" footer). Leave the base moulding and all four doors uncut for now; both get measured off the real cabinet later. Allowing about 1/8" per saw cut, the 1x12 parts need FIVE 8 ft boards, not the four the materials list declares — no two of them pair on a 96" board, since the shortest is 31-1/2" and the longest offcut is 26-1/2". Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

ops.title: Build the carcass
ops.body: Assemble the two 35-1/4" sides, the 69-1/2" bottom shelf, the 31-1/2" center divider and the 72" top into a box. The bottom shelf runs between the sides, so the carcass finishes 71" wide — which is why the face frame header and the back panel are both 71", and why the 72" top overhangs just 1/2" at each end. Drill the pocket holes for the face frame NOW, before assembly, with the 3/4" setting and the holes facing forward: once the face frame is on and the back is closed you cannot get a drill in there. Glue and screw with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, and check the diagonals before the glue sets.

ops.title: Build and attach the face frame
ops.body: Build the frame flat on the bench before it goes anywhere near the cabinet: the two 32-3/4" sides stand full height, the 71" header runs across the top of them, and the 66" footer fits between them at the bottom — 71" less two 2-1/2" sides is exactly 66". Then set the 30-1/4" center divider trim between the header and footer at the midpoint, which leaves two openings of 31-3/4" wide by 30-1/4" tall. Join it all with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws, check it for square across the diagonals, and only then glue and pin it to the front of the carcass with 1-1/4" brad nails. The frame has to go on before the back does, so you still have drill access inside.

ops.title: Add interior shelving
ops.body: The two 34-3/8" interior shelves are cut to exactly half the interior: 69-1/2" between the sides, less the 3/4" center divider, halved. Set them on shelf pins if you want them adjustable, or fix them with pocket holes drilled on their undersides where nothing will see them. Whichever you choose, hold both shelves at the same height off the bottom in each bay — with inset doors the shelf line shows through the opening every time a door is open, and a mismatch reads immediately.

ops.title: Add base trim
ops.body: Measure the finished base and cut the moulding to it rather than to the cut list's four 20" pieces — the base is 72" across the front and 11-1/4" deep on each side, which needs roughly 94-1/2" of moulding plus waste at each miter, so the single 8 ft stick declared will not go all the way around and four 20" pieces come nowhere close. Cut outside miters at the two front corners, glue and pin the front piece on first, then fit each return to it. Hold the top edge of the moulding to one consistent line off the floor; a base trim that wanders is more obvious than an uneven door gap.

ops.title: Build and hang the doors
ops.body: Measure your two openings directly before cutting any door part; built to plan they are 31-3/4" wide by 30-1/4" tall, but real 1x3 width varies and the openings are what rule. Be aware that the plan is internally inconsistent here: it gives TWO openings but lists four doors, four pairs of hinges and four knobs at 15-1/2" x 15" each, which is neither a full nor a half opening with any consistent reveal — decide whether you are hanging one door per opening or a pair, and size accordingly. Whichever you choose, build each door 1/4" less than the space it fills in both width and height, which nets an even 1/8" gap on all four sides once hinged. Hang them, adjust until every gap reads the same, and add a magnetic catch if your hinges do not hold the doors shut on their own.

flags: DRAWER SIDES QUANTITY IS HALF WHAT IS NEEDED: six 1x10 drawer sides are listed for six drawers, which need twelve. With twelve, the 1x10 requirement is 147" of sides plus 204" of fronts and backs = 351", which packs into FOUR 96" boards with zero slack — 32 linear feet against the 24 ft declared. Provable shortfall.

THE DRAWER BOX PARTS DO NOT FIT THE BAYS, and this is not resolvable from the plan's data. The bays are 13-1/2" wide: 43-1/2" between the face frame verticals, less two 1-1/2" mid uprights, over three bays. That is confirmed independently by the drawer faces, which are 13-1/4" x 11-1/4" and give exactly the 1/8" reveal the plan calls for against a 13-1/2" x 11-1/2" opening. But the drawer front and back pieces are cut at 17" and the bottoms at 17-3/4" — several inches wider than the bay they must sit in. Neither reading of the box closes against the 17-3/4" x 13-1/4" bottom either (17 + 1-1/2 = 18-1/2, 12-1/4 + 1-1/2 = 13-3/4). Flagged and written around; no joint was invented to absorb it.
MID UPRIGHTS ARE HALF-COUNTED TOO: three bays per row needs two uprights, and two rows in each of two frames is four rows — eight uprights. Four are listed.

Verified sound and used: the case is 50-1/2" wide (43-1/2" plus two 3-1/2" verticals) and the top is six 47-1/2" 2x4 laid up to 21" deep between two 21" end boards, giving 50-1/2" x 21" — flush at the ends, 3/4" proud front and back over the 19-1/2" deep case (16-1/2" rails plus two 1-1/2" frames). The three 5-1/2" side panels total 16-1/2", matching the connecting rails as the cut list note says.
2x4 (8 of 8), 2x2 (3 of 3), 1x2 (3 of 3) and 1x6 (2 of 2) all verified sufficient — no shortfall claimed on those.

The old step 7 was left alone in substance: it never told the builder to cover the side panel joints, which is good, because those joints run vertically for 29-1/2" and the 16-1/2" trim runs perpendicular and could not cover them.

ops.title: Cut all the parts
ops.body: Cut four face frame verticals at 31", two face frame drawer horizontals at 43-1/2", ten connecting rails at 16-1/2", six top boards at 47-1/2" and two top end boards at 21" from the 2x4. Cut four face frame top/bottom horizontals at 43-1/2" and the mid uprights at 11-1/2" from the 2x2 — note the cut list says four uprights, but three bays per row needs two, and there are two rows in each of the two frames, so cut EIGHT. Cut eight drawer guides at 16-1/2" and four side trim at 16-1/2" from the 1x2. Cut six side panels at 29-1/2" from the 1x6, and two side trim at 16-1/2" from the 1x4. From the 1x10 cut TWELVE drawer sides at 12-1/4" and twelve drawer fronts and backs at 17" — twelve sides, not the six the cut list shows, since six boxes need two apiece; that is 351" of 1x10, which fills four 8 ft boards with nothing to spare, so buy 32 linear feet rather than the 24 declared. Cut six drawer bottoms at 13-1/4" from the 1/4" plywood and six drawer faces at 13-1/4" from the 1x12. Hold the drawer parts back from final size until you have measured a real bay in step 5 — the listed widths do not fit the openings this frame produces. Square every cut and label each piece.

ops.title: Build the two face frames
ops.body: Each frame is two 31" 2x4 verticals with the 43-1/2" horizontals between them: a 2x2 across the top, a 2x2 across the bottom, and the 3-1/2" 2x4 drawer horizontal splitting the frame into two drawer rows. Two 11-1/2" 2x2 mid uprights in each row divide it into three bays — 43-1/2" less two 1-1/2" uprights, over three, is 13-1/2" per bay, which is what the 13-1/4" drawer faces are sized to leave an even 1/8" reveal against. Build both frames side by side on the same flat surface, using pocket holes throughout, and lay one on the other before you join anything: mismatched frames are the single thing that makes these hardware-free drawers run cockeyed, and there is no slide to forgive it. Drill upward-facing pocket holes along the inside of the top horizontals now, for attaching the top much later.

ops.title: Join the frames
ops.body: Stand the two frames parallel and join them with the ten 16-1/2" connecting rails, running front to back — one pair at each end and one pair at each mid upright, so every bay has a rail down each side to carry a guide. That 16-1/2" span plus the 1-1/2" thickness of each frame makes the case 19-1/2" deep, and 43-1/2" plus two 3-1/2" verticals makes it 50-1/2" wide. Sight down the case from one end as you fasten: the two frames have to end up directly in line, not just parallel, or every drawer will run out of square with its own opening.

ops.title: Add the drawer guides
ops.body: Screw the eight 16-1/2" 1x2 guides flat on top of the connecting rails, one down each side of every bay, square to the front face frame — a guide that toes in even slightly will bind a wood-on-wood drawer at the back of its travel. Use only TWO screws per guide and no glue. That is deliberate under-fastening, not an oversight: with no slide hardware to adjust, moving a guide 1/16" is the only correction available once a drawer binds, and you want to be able to back the screws out and shift it.

ops.title: Build the drawer boxes
ops.body: Measure a real bay before cutting anything to final size. Built to plan the openings are 13-1/2" wide by 11-1/2" tall, and the rule is to build each box 1/4" under in both directions — 13-1/4" by 11-1/4" — leaving a consistent 1/8" gap all round. Be warned that the cut list disagrees with its own frame here: the drawer fronts and backs are drawn at 17" and the bottoms at 17-3/4", both several inches wider than the 13-1/2" bay they must enter, and the box does not close against that bottom in either orientation. Cut the fronts and backs to your measured opening less 1/4", run the 12-1/4" sides front to back inside the 16-1/2" case depth, and cut each bottom to the box you actually built rather than to the listed size. Assemble with glue and 1-1/4" brads, checking each box for square — too tight and a bare wood drawer binds on the first pull, with no metal slide to hide the error.

ops.title: Attach the side panels
ops.body: Slide every drawer in and out through its full travel before the sides go on — once the panels are fastened you lose the access that lets you reach in and shift a guide. When you are satisfied, attach three 29-1/2" 1x6 side panels to each end of the case, standing them on edge next to each other; three 5-1/2" boards make 16-1/2", which is exactly the depth of the connecting rails they land on. Fasten through pocket holes into the frame verticals and rails so nothing shows on the outside face.

ops.title: Trim the side panels
ops.body: Trim each side with the 16-1/2" 1x2 and 1x4 pieces so the ends read like the front — the 1x4 gives a wider band and the 1x2 a narrower one, and both are cut to the 16-1/2" side depth. Hold them to the same lines as the front face frame's horizontals so the trim wraps the corner instead of stopping at a random height. Glue and pin with 1-1/4" brads, then set the nails and fill.

ops.title: Build and attach the top
ops.body: Build the top flat on the bench as its own slab: six 47-1/2" 2x4 laid side by side make 21", with a 21" end board capping each end, so the finished top is 50-1/2" wide by 21" deep. Join it with pocket holes and glue while it is off the dresser — keeping a slab this size flat is far easier on a bench than fighting clamps and gravity on top of a standing case. Set it on the dresser and pull it down through the upward-facing pocket holes you drilled in the frames: 50-1/2" matches the case width exactly, so it lands flush at both ends and stands 3/4" proud front and back over the 19-1/2" case. Then attach the six drawer faces over the closed boxes on 1/8" spacers, fit the pulls, sand and finish.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

baseStepTitles: Build the headboard and footboard panel assemblies
Add the bottom and top supports and cap
Install the drawer boxes and faces
Attach the storage boxes and finish
flags: STORAGE BOX BACK SIZE IS WRONG: listed at 75" x 75", but the box is 15" tall — the 13-1/2" top/bottom/divider pieces plus a 3/4" panel above and below — so each back is 75" x 15". A 75" x 75" panel does not even come out of a 48"-wide sheet, and two of them would consume far more than the single 1/4" sheet declared. Corrected in the prose.

THE DRAWER BOX ROWS ARE LABELLED BACKWARDS, stated once in step 5 and not repeated. Box geometry proves it: the 14" pieces run the full length and the 21-1/2" pieces sit between them, confirmed by the 23" x 14" bottom. So the 21-1/2" pieces (labelled 'sides') are the front and back, running the 23" width, and the 14" pieces (labelled 'front/back') are the sides, running the 14" slide depth.

THE MATTRESS SLAT SYSTEM IS IN THE MATERIALS BUT NOT IN THE CUT LIST: three 8 ft 2x2 and nine 8 ft 1x3 are declared, and the 2x6 note calls the caps 'part of the slat system', yet no cutList row produces a slat or a ledger. Named in the final step so it is not silently skipped; no dimensions invented for it.
The horizontal drawer-face trim is listed at 24" against a 23-3/4" face — cut it to the assembled face rather than the listed length.

Verified correct and now stated: the 39" 2x4 supports span the full outside width (32" panel plus two 3-1/2" posts), and the 41" 2x6 caps centered on them give exactly the 1" overhang on all sides the description promises (5-1/2" cap over a 3-1/2" post is 1" front and back). Each storage box has three 24" bays (75" less four 3/4" pieces = 72", over three). Drawer boxes at 23" wide in a 24" opening leave 1/2" per side for the 14" Euro slides, and the 23-3/4" faces leave a 1/8" reveal each side.
All lumber verified SUFFICIENT — 1x10 (5 of 5), 2x4 (2 of 3 10-footers), 2x6 (2 of 2), 1x4 (2 of 2), 4x4 (2 of 2). No shortfall claimed.

ops.title: Add the bottom and top supports and cap
ops.body: Drill two 1-1/2" pocket holes in each end of the 39" 2x4 supports and attach one low between the headboard posts and one between the footboard posts with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws and glue. That 39" is the full outside width — the 32" panel plus a 3-1/2" post at each end — so these run across the outside faces of the posts rather than between them. Attach the 32" 2x4 top rail pair to the headboard through the pocket holes you drilled in the panel with 1-1/4" screws. Then cap both headboard and footboard with a 41" 2x6, fastened with the 1-1/2" to 2-1/2" self-tapping screws and glue: centered on the 39" width it stands 1" proud at each end, and a 5-1/2" cap over a 3-1/2" post is 1" proud front and back — the 1" overhang on all sides the design calls for.

ops.title: Build the storage boxes
ops.body: If you are staining, edge-band the front edges of every 3/4" plywood piece now — plywood edge grain drinks stain unevenly and reads as a stripe. Each box is two 75" x 15-3/4" panels top and bottom with four 13-1/2" pieces standing between them: one at each end and two dividers, which leaves three bays of 24" (75" less four 3/4" pieces is 72", over three). Those two 13-1/2" pieces plus a 3/4" panel above and below make the box 15" tall, so cut each 1/4" back to 75" x 15" — NOT the 75" x 75" the cut list shows, which will not even come out of a 48"-wide sheet. Take real care getting the boxes square: this box does the structural job of a siderail and also has to run a drawer on cheap slides, and a rack that would be cosmetic on a bookshelf jams a drawer here every single time. If your drill will not fit inside for a second row of pocket holes, nail that side with 1-1/4" brads and glue; attach the back with 3/4" brads and glue, checking square right before it goes on.

ops.title: Build the optional drawer boxes
ops.body: Measure the actual bay in your assembled storage box first. Built to plan it is 24" wide, and the drawer is 23" wide over its outside faces, leaving 1/2" per side for the 14" Euro slides; if your bay is not 24", adjust the box and not the other way round. Note that the cut list's two drawer rows are labelled backwards — the 21-1/2" pieces are the front and back, running the 23" width, and the 14" pieces are the sides, running the slide depth. Assembled that way the box is 21-1/2" plus two 3/4" ends = 23" wide by 14" deep, which is exactly what the 23" x 14" bottom is cut for. Build all six with the pocket holes on the outside faces, where the drawer face hides them.

ops.title: Install the drawer boxes and faces
ops.body: Mount the 14" slides in each bay and set the boxes in inset 3/4" from the outside face, so the drawer faces sit proud of the box rather than flush with it. Each 23-3/4" face in a 24" opening leaves 1/8" at each side — center it on 1/8" spacers over the closed box and fix it from inside the box so no fastener shows on the face. If you want the paneled look, add the 1/4" hobby-stock trim around each face, cutting the horizontal pieces to the face you actually built rather than the listed 24", which is 1/4" longer than the 23-3/4" face. Fit the knobs or handles last, after the faces are all aligned with each other.

ops.title: Attach the storage boxes, add the mattress support, and finish
ops.body: Stand a storage box along each side of the bed and fasten it to the headboard and footboard with the 1-1/2" to 2-1/2" self-tapping screws — the boxes are doing the job a siderail normally does, so drive into solid material and not just plywood edge. Where a box meets the footboard you may need shims between them to hold everything aligned and square; check the two diagonals across the bed opening before you commit. The mattress support is the one thing this plan lists but never dimensions: the 2x2 and 1x3 in the materials are there for slat ledgers and slats, so cut ledgers to run the length of each box's inside face and slats to span between them, sized to your mattress. Then sand the whole bed, fill any visible fastener holes, and finish before the mattress goes in.
